item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can we at least agree that it's not a sphere? :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only format-screw that I've enjoyed so far. My only question is: If the scip is a concept, rather than a physical object, what did they put in the pond?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No
Thank you

You don't need to feel guilty about this. It was a remark that I needed to point out. If you didn't, then I would have needed to do the same with other similar remarks, but this time on the discussion page, not in the chat or the crit forums.

Your overall feedback was greatly appreciated.

Plus, you weren't the only one that made the cognitohazard/memetic/infohazard error. Plenty of people did the same.

SCP-3463 got an amazing rating and I partly have to thank you for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was my fault.
Sorry about that

Back when I was a terrible critic, I made the comparison to SCP-2950 for three reasons:

  1. I had just read it
  2. I thought that the ideas were similar (a thing that changes depending on what you think it is vs. a thing that changes what you think it is)
  3. I'm an idiot

I'm also to blame for it being purely labeled as a cognitohazard instead of as a memetic effect and cognitohazard, for pretty much the same reasons as above.

Overall, my terrible noob-advice is probably why this scip didn't do that well.

item.title: Best Footnote
item.content:encoded:
This was pretty cool. I'm gonna give it a +1.

On a side note, this is the best footnote on the entire site:

The object did not murder D-8956's family, nor is it red. It is in fact, blue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All that said, I am a fan of 3463. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can't the Foundation expose a naive individual (someone without any knowledge of 3463) to the object, and have them describe it?

If I understand the mechanism correctly, they should see either an empty box, or a [DATA CORRUPTED].

If they say "the box is empty"… That's a statement about the box, not about 3463 - so the interviewers should either accuse the viewer of lying…

"The box isn't empty, it contains 3463!"
"Oh, that's right, the box wasn't empty at all."

OR not lying - "How does an inanimate object breach containment! Where did it go!?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably because of the [SYSTEM ERROR] so it might be just some glitchy computer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How come we get the password wrong and still have access?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really understand why we needed all the extra iterations; I don't feel like I'm discovering more information when I read them. Very bluntly and formulaicly going "it's a memetic anomaly itself" feels like something we could guess on our own. As far as I'm concerned, this actually works way better as only the current iteration. We can imagine the possibilities on our own, and it's scarier when nobody in-universe states that something is wrong but it's obvious to us.

item.title: Re: Pure Keter, indeed!
item.content:encoded: This gave me an idea: We could have one of the iterations say it was responsible for the death of the entire population of a subcontinent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This sort of reminds me of SCP-1184, in the way that the Foundation still isn't entirely sure about its details, due to its abilities.

Also, this ability is extremely similar to 1184, and could be abused in a similar fashion.
To quote the author, SpookyPizza: "if somebody walked up to you and said that SCP-3463 is god and made water undrinkable then you would believe it"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm afraid I'm not sold on this. I was quite excited by the basic idea, but the article just reiterates the starting premise ("we can never truly know what this is - what can you trust?") rather than taking it anywhere as a story. There is a big risk with "memetic" / "infohazard" SCPs that they end up sounding much the same - the reason that articles like SCP-1539 and SCP-3002 work is that they take the anomaly and show it in action, or add a personal story to the mix. Simply throwing a few login details (which were necessary why?) and examples of the errors that the Foundation has made over the years doesn't do enough to draw me in, or exhibit the potential that this anomaly has to surprise me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very intriguing SCP! So to summarize, this SCP is an object or entity that nobody is truly certain what it actually is, because of its anomalous effects? Sorry that I don't have more of a detailed reason why I like this article, but heck, I just like this article! (+1)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like I've seen all the elements of this in other skips already. The presentation is very blunt and straightforward about the "Here's what's actually going on" part. Plus, it could use a lot of proofreading. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know how that slipped in there. Anyway it's fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr J███, an ex-employee from the brand "National" once told "All previous information about SCP-3463 is false" to Dr J███.

Did Dr J███ tell himself, or someone who has a confusingly similar name?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But isn't that the default for how passwords should be set? Does that mean that this particular password has to be especially complex?

I took care of that.

I think it might be better to directly show the different iterations.

I might implement this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Documentation concerning SCP-3463 must be guarded with a complex password.

But isn't that the default for how passwords should be set? Does that mean that this particular password has to be especially complex?

Furthermore, the framing of failed access and then a sudden allowance of access seems odd. Based on the article, I think it might be better to directly show the different iterations. I don't understand how the failed access routine conveys complex password. It more likely conveys someone who cannot remember the password to the system, and did not consider hacking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So there is still an extremely small slimmer of hope of knowing the truth about SCP-3463 despite its effects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's to showcase one of the containment procedures AKA the password.
It also allows me to showcase the history of the file.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, I can see its unique points. However, I must ask about the presentation. Why start from SCPSYSTEM? What does it do for the article as a whole?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why does the effect of SCP-3463 last five years?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It reminds me more of SCP-2330 than SCP-2950, but the core of the idea and resulting risks and implications are different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haven't read the article yet, but how similar is this to SCP-2950?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awesome idea!!! Formatting could use a little bit more work, but besides that, best new SCP I've read in quite a while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Are people with speech and language disabilities not allowed near this one because, as one researcher says, "Any lie or mistake we make will cost us the truth"? If so, that's a neat way to approach a cognitohazard/memetic anomaly (I hope I'm using those words right, I'm new here).

I like the idea a lot, but similar to what Roget said, I think it could use a little more oomf - just a little more tease of why SCP-3463 is so potentially dangerous. Otherwise, it's certainly a cool twist. +1

Also, would you be open to allowing me to edit some of the spelling/grammar mistakes? I'd like to get my feet wet, if at all possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've got my head wrapped around the concept, it reminds me of that God damn toaster. But I never really feel like the other shoe drops, or that the article does anything other than reiterate its central ideas. It feels like two-thirds of a complete article. It also kinda skirts around how much of a mindfuck this is with some of the more rigid parameters of its effect, I never really got an idea of how it could infest the human mind the way the goddamn toaster and similar articles do. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No vote for now; I love the concept, but I noticed a lot of typos/clunky phrasing issues. I won't bother mentioning any specifics in case that was deliberate as a representation of the [DATA CORRUPTED] I noticed (automatic +1 if it was).

item.title: Pure Keter, indeed!
item.content:encoded:
The opening password entry sequence really piqued my interest and the information that followed certainly didn't disappoint. The various 'iterations' of SCP-3463 were clever and the interview log demonstrated the potentially dangerous effects from exposure - and all from a thought.
What I also really enjoyed is that the SCP wasn't at any point 'SCP-3463 was responsible for the death of an entire population of a subcontinent' - but it COULD be. It didn't have to be shock horror, it in itself leaves the reader dwelling on all the horrifying possibilities for its memetic spread.
Clever stuff. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hope all prospective contributors read this and realize that a SCP does not have to be a Thing What Kills You.

This was well thought out and presented. It took me a couple of read throughs to fully get, but it was worth it. I'll remember this the next time I see a blue fan.

item.title: Beautifully done
item.content:encoded:
This is a complete mindf*** and I love it. The format/layout of this makes the reader explore and discover more about this SCP. It's a unique entity. Even when you think you have it figured out, you're proven wrong to the point where you have to accept your complete lack of understanding of SCP-3463. Even the very end has me questioning if its correct or not, and leaves me to wonder about it. It makes a connection to the reader, as the supposed effects pertain to reading it. Lastly, this entity is a pure Keter. There is no hope of completely containing it, or at all. Its very existence poses a threat.

Overall, it's incredibly well done.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very interesting concept. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, this is SCP-3463. I hope you find it nice (Please find it nice) and if you find it nice then it's that's nice and thank you.

+ Show FAQ
- Hide FAQ

SCP-3463 isn't a fan. If you've read update-A and version 1 of the file, you'd know that it's instead a memetic pinocchio.

SCP-3463 is Keter if somebody walked up to you and said that SCP-3463 is god and made water undrinkable then you would believe it even though water is still drinkable and SCP-3463 is not god. You would also tell everyone that because hey! God is a sadistic ventilator that made water undrinkable and we're all gonna die!

The Foundation doesn't want you to believe that it's a fan, it believes that it's a fan even though it's not.

I've switched between cognitohazard and infohazard 7 times already because people told me it was either an infohazard or a cognitohazard and I'm using cognitohazard because it's way broader than infohazard. It might as well be both at the same time.

I tried the paradox thing with this SCP and people didn't like it because it turned into a thing that makes you insane.

Handing it to the O5 Council is overkill. That's why five years later after update-255, they immediately got back to the old containment procedures. Yes, Dr U made a mistake, no that's not lolfoundation-y, he's just a human being who can make errors, just like every other person who worked on this.

Banning suggestions and new information is a double-edged sword. What if their knowledge of SCP-3463 was wrong?

No, SCP-3463 is not SCP-2950, SCP-2950 changes its form through perception of it. SCP-3463 never changes its form, it's only its appearance through errors among people that changes.

Thanks to everyone who gave me feedback, really appreciated.
